SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (two projection-based approaches and co-decoding improvements) are supported by experimental results on in-house and CoNLL datasets and comparisons to prior work (e.g., Täckström et al. (2012) and Tsai et al. (2016)). Mathematical components are simple and clearly stated (e.g., the sentence quality score q(y) in Eq. (5) and the linear mapping M_fe for embedding projection). The paper does make heuristic assumptions (e.g., that independent errors from the English NER and alignment systems are unlikely to produce consistent wrong tags) that are plausible but not formally justified, and no statistical significance tests are reported in the excerpt.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The combination of (i) a heuristic data-selection scheme for annotation projection, (ii) direct representation projection mapping all target languages into a single English embedding space, and (iii) co-decoding of the two projections is a novel integration of ideas. While the components build on existing work (e.g., embedding mappings from Mikolov et al. and annotation projection), the paper emphasizes a practical single-universal-NER transfer via mapping all languages to English, which differentiates it from prior pairwise or jointly trained cross-lingual embedding approaches.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized: it describes models (NN1/NN2), the annotation-quality metric (Table 1 and Eq. (5)), representation projection (Section 4) and experiments (Section 6) in a logical order. Notation and key formulas (e.g., q(y) and the linear mapping M_fe) are defined, and figures/tables (referenced) are used to illustrate design choices. Some implementation details are brief in the excerpt (e.g., exact NN hyperparameters beyond layer types), but overall the methods and experimental setup are communicated clearly.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments cover multiple languages and datasets (in-house and CoNLL) and compare AP, representation projection (NN1/NN2) and co-decoding schemes, showing ablation between NN1 and NN2 and AP vs projection. However, the excerpt lacks deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of thresholds q and n, robustness to mapping quality), failure-case discussion, and full reproducibility details (some training settings are given but many hyperparameters and alignment procedures are not fully specified).

IMPACT.justification: Cross-lingual NER without target-language annotation addresses an important practical problem, and the proposal to reuse a single English NER via embedding mappings could influence how practitioners scale NER to many languages. The reported gains over prior cross-lingual approaches on CoNLL test data suggest useful improvements that others may build upon, although the work is an incremental step rather than transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (cross-lingual, weakly supervised NER) and the methods (embedding projection, annotation projection, neural sequence models) are squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML conferences. The paper uses standard datasets (CoNLL) and evaluation practices expected at such venues and positions itself relative to relevant prior work.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its best systems to prior cross-lingual NER methods (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on CoNLL test sets and reports where it outperforms them. Comparisons include multiple internal baselines (AP, NN1, NN2) and co-decoding variants, but the excerpt does not show statistical testing or exhaustive comparisons to all recent alternatives, and some experimental condition details (e.g., exact data preprocessing/alignment) that affect fairness are not fully described.
